hero_image.caption: Charles W. Chesnutt.
hero_image.license: Public domain in the United States (published before January 1, 1931; copyright expired)

inline_images.alt: Wood engraving The First Vote, showing African American men waiting in line to cast ballots, Harper's Weekly, 1867
inline_images.caption: The First Vote (Harper's Weekly, 1867) depicts Black men exercising the franchise during Reconstruction, the very right whose legal stripping Charles W. Chesnutt documents in his 1903 essay on disfranchisement.
inline_images.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division

related_chapters.subject: Ellie Dahmer
related_chapters.chapter_title: Read That and Tell Me What It Means
related_chapters.topic: Registrar Lynd and the Constitution Test
related_chapters.why: Chesnutt details the understanding clause that let registrars reject Black applicants at will, and Dahmer describes registrar Theron Lynd's constitution-interpretation test in Hattiesburg.

related_chapters.subject: Clarence Magee
related_chapters.chapter_title: The Cat-and-Mouse Test, and the Lawsuit
related_chapters.why: Chesnutt's remedy for disfranchisement runs through the courts, and Magee recounts the cat-and-mouse registration test and the lawsuit brought against it.

related_chapters.subject: Grace Hall Miller
related_chapters.why: Chesnutt warns that disfranchisement leaves a people defenseless and exposed to reprisal, and Miller recalls being among the first two to register in Baker County and the retaliation that followed.

related_chapters.subject: Rosie Head
related_chapters.chapter_title: They Never Told Us We Had Passed
related_chapters.why: Chesnutt describes ingenious local machinery built to keep the ballot from Black citizens, and Head recounts being held up and never told she had passed until federal registrars arrived.

related_chapters.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
related_chapters.chapter_title: I Collected Poll Tax in My Beauty Shop
related_chapters.why: Chesnutt itemizes the poll tax among the property qualifications used to bar the vote, and Connor, later a redistricting-suit plaintiff, describes paying and collecting poll tax in her beauty shop.

related_chapters.subject: Timothy Jenkins
related_chapters.why: Chesnutt holds that the direct remedy lies through political and legal action, and Jenkins, a SNCC strategist and lawyer, recounts winning the voting cases before a three-judge panel.

top_voice.subject: Timothy Jenkins
top_voice.reason: Chesnutt argues the disfranchised must answer through the courts, Congress, and public opinion, and Jenkins is the SNCC strategist and Howard-trained lawyer who built the legal architecture of voting rights described in his testimony.

closing_image.alt: Timothy Lionel Jenkins, SNCC veteran, photographed in 2016
closing_image.caption: Timothy Jenkins, the corpus voice this essay connects to most. Chesnutt argues the disfranchised must answer through the courts, Congress, and public opinion, and Jenkins is the SNCC strategist and Howard-trained lawyer who built the legal architecture of voting rights described in his testimony.

connections.summary: Across the Civil Rights History Project, the oral histories below take up the questions The Disfranchisement of the Negro raises. Our embedding model places it nearest the testimony of Ruby Sales, Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones, and Amos C. Brown.
